ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for chicken tikka; A0CKM (chicken meat, grilled) is the closest defensible parent category covering marinated grilled chicken preparations.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of chicken tikka is approximately 200 g, comprising 4 to 5 boneless pieces of marinated grilled chicken, typically served with mint chutney and sliced onion.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Tikka — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (ICMR-NIN, 2017 revised edition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, roasted (NDB 05013) used for micronutrient cross-reference
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides the primary macro and sodium values for tandoori/tikka-style grilled chicken. Micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamins) are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked boneless chicken breast (NDB 05013) as the closest approved secondary source. Values represent a composite of plain, malai, achari, and hariyali variants; plain tikka macros are used as the base since malai adds cream (slightly higher fat) and hariyali/achari add herb/spice pastes with negligible macro impact at serving level.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021; analogous estimate based on grilled marinated chicken (protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate food)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Chicken tikka is a protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate grilled food (approximately 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g). The University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 international GI tables do not list chicken tikka directly. A GI of 30 is assigned by analogy with other grilled/tandoori chicken preparations and protein-rich low-carbohydrate foods, consistent with the established principle that high-protein, low-carbohydrate foods have low GI values. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (30 × 5.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade and spice rub
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving of chicken tikka contributes approximately 640 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt marinades and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken tikka has a low glycemic index (approximately 30) and very low carbohydrate content (~2.5 g per 100 g), making it a suitable high-protein option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as naan or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein with moderate saturated fat; preparation method avoids deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chicken tikka is grilled or tandoor-cooked rather than deep-fried, resulting in moderate saturated fat (~1.8 g per 100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer plain or hariyali variants over malai tikka, which contains additional cream and higher saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken tikka is a classic North Indian dish consisting of boneless chicken pieces marinated in spiced yoghurt and cooked in a tandoor (clay oven) or over a charcoal grill. The marinade typically includes yoghurt, lemon juice, ginger-garlic paste, cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli powder, and garam masala, which tenderise the meat and impart a characteristic smoky, tangy, and mildly spiced flavour. The dish is served in several popular variants: plain tikka (standard spiced marinade), malai tikka (cream and cheese-enriched marinade for a richer, milder flavour), achari tikka (pickled spice marinade with mustard seeds, fenugreek, and fennel for a tangy, pungent profile), and hariyali tikka (green herb marinade with coriander, mint, and green chilli for a fresh, vibrant flavour). Nutritionally, chicken tikka is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food with approximately 25 g protein and 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it suitable for low-glycaemic and high-protein dietary patterns. The glycemic index is low (approximately 30), and a standard 200 g serving contributes a glycemic load of only 1.5. Sodium content is moderate (~320 mg per 100 g) due to the spiced marinade. Chicken tikka is widely consumed in Indian restaurants across Singapore and is a popular starter or main course. It is halal-certified in most Indian Muslim restaurants and is gluten-free by default. Clinically, it is a favourable protein source for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease, though sodium intake should be monitored for those with hypertension.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken tikka?
Is chicken tikka good for diabetics?
What is the difference between plain, malai, achari, and hariyali chicken tikka?

confidence_reason: Primary macro values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for tandoori/tikka-style grilled chicken, which is the closest approved regional source. Micronutrient values are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked boneless chicken. No single approved source provides an exact entry for 'Chicken Tikka (Plain/Malai/Achari/Hariyali)' as a composite dish; values represent the plain tikka base. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework for protein-dominant low-carbohydrate grilled foods. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. This record covers four variants of chicken tikka served at Dugout - Circular Road, Singapore (field survey): plain, malai, achari, and hariyali. Nutrient values represent the plain tikka base; malai tikka will have higher fat and energy due to cream addition. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category; USDA SR Legacy reports negligible vitamin D for cooked chicken breast. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed as effectively zero for grilled chicken preparations per USDA FoodData Central (no artificial trans fats; negligible ruminant trans fats below detection threshold in poultry). 4. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for chicken tikka was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. 5. Sodium value of 320 mg per 100 g reflects a typical restaurant-style marinade; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. 6. The food is classified as halal-suitable based on the use of chicken and standard spice ingredients; halal certification depends on the specific restaurant. 7. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this is an Indian dish served in Singapore. 8. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy for cooked chicken breast with oil-based marinade.
